snippet.title: The Supreme Court Case That Put a Warrant on Your Phone
snippet.description:
A police officer arrests you and takes the phone from your pocket. Can he open it without a warrant?

This episode explains Riley v. California, the 2014 Supreme Court case that changed how the Fourth Amendment applies to the device most people carry everywhere. The Court had to decide whether a phone is just another item in your pocket, or something different in kind: messages, photos, location history, apps, and the private record of a life.

We trace David Riley's traffic stop, the older search-incident-to-arrest rule, the unanimous Supreme Court decision, and why the phrase "get a warrant" became the new baseline for phone searches after arrest.

This is an educational documentary, not legal advice.

Visual note: this video uses AI-generated symbolic reconstructions and motion graphics. They are not authentic footage, and no real-person likeness is intended.

Next episode: the location trail your phone leaves behind.

Chapters:
00:00 Can police open your phone?
00:30 The old pocket-search rule
01:15 David Riley's arrest
03:30 Search incident to arrest
06:15 Get a warrant
09:00 Why smartphones changed the rule
10:45 Next: location records
